title: Monitoring & Observability
subtitle: Metrics, logs, traces, alerts, and knowing what your app is doing in production

sections.title: The Three Pillars of Observability
sections.text:
When your app is in production, you can't just attach a debugger. You need observability — the ability to understand what's happening from the outside. Three pillars:

  • Metrics — Numbers over time. CPU usage, request rate, error rate, latency percentiles. "How many requests per second?" Answers: system health and trends.
  • Logs — Individual events with timestamps. "What happened at 3:14 PM?" Answers: what went wrong and where. Structured JSON logs with fields, not plain text.
  • Traces — Track a single request across multiple services. Shows you where time is spent. "Why did this API call take 3 seconds?" Answers: which service was slow.

You don't need all three on day one. Start with logs and basic metrics (request rate, error rate, p99 latency). Add traces when you have microservices.

items.heading: Metrics
items.body: Numbers over time: req/s, error rate, CPU, p99 latency.

items.heading: Logs
items.body: Structured events with timestamps and context.

sections.caption: The three pillars: metrics tell you WHAT, logs tell you WHY, traces tell you WHERE
sections.callout: The p99 latency is the most important metric — it tells you the experience of your slowest 1% of users. If p99 is 5 seconds, 1 in 100 users has a terrible experience.

sections.title: Structured Logging
sections.text:
Don't write console.log('User logged in', userId). That's a string you'll have to regex-parse later. Write structured logs — JSON objects with fields:

{"level":"info","msg":"user logged in","userId":42,"duration":120,"reqId":"abc-123"}

Now you can search, filter, and aggregate: "Show me all errors in the last hour with duration > 1s" → level:error AND duration:>1000.

Tools: Pino (fast JSON logger for Node), Winston (more features), Bunyan (legacy). All output JSON. Pair with a log aggregator: ELK (Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana), Grafana Loki, or Datadog.

Always include: level, timestamp, msg, reqId (trace ID), service. Add context like userId, orderId, duration.
sections.caption: Structured logs flow from your app to a central aggregator for search and alerting
sections.callout: Always redact passwords, tokens, and PII from logs. Pino has a built-in redact option. A log leak is a security breach.

sections.title: Alerting & Incident Response
sections.text:
Better to wake up once a month for a real emergency than every night for false alarms. Tune your alerts:

  • Page on symptoms, not causes — Alert when error rate spikes, not when a specific error appears. High error rate is a symptom. A single error might be noise.
  • Use thresholds with duration — "Error rate > 5% for 5 minutes" not "Error rate > 5% instantly". Short spikes resolve themselves.
  • Burn rate alerting — Alert when you're burning through your error budget faster than expected. 99.9% SLO means 0.1% errors allowed over a month. If you hit 0.05% in one hour, you're on track to breach — page now.

When something breaks: Stop → Assess → Fix → Learn. Don't panic-deploy. Check the dashboard. Read the logs. Understand the scope. Then fix. After the fix, write a postmortem: what happened, why, how it was fixed, how to prevent it.
sections.caption: Incident response: stop → assess → fix → learn. Don't skip the postmortem.
sections.code:
# Burn rate alerting (PromQL)
# Alert if error rate is above 5% for 5 minutes
- alert: HighErrorRate
  expr: |
    rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])
    /
    rate(http_requests_total[5m]) > 0.05
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: critical
  annotations:
    summary: "Error rate above 5% for 5 minutes"
    description: "Current error rate: {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"

# Postmortem template
## Summary
- **Date**:
- **Duration**:
- **Impact**: X users affected, Y revenue lost

## Timeline
- 14:00 — Deployed v2.3.1
- 14:03 — Alert fired (error rate spike)
- 14:05 — Rollback initiated
- 14:08 — Monitoring confirms recovery

## Root Cause
- Missing null check in payment processing
- Only affected users with empty discount codes

## Action Items
- [ ] Add null check and unit test
- [ ] Add integration test for payment flow
- [ ] Add canary deployment step to CI
sections.callout: Every incident should have a blameless postmortem. The question isn't 'who did this' but 'what system failure allowed this to happen'. Fix the process, not the person.
